data.title: Deutsche Boerse Takes $200 Million Stake in Crypto Platform Kraken
data.description:
By Adria Calatayud 




  Deutsche Boerse said it is investing $200 million in Payward, taking a stake in the company behind U.S. cryptocurrency platform Kraken to deepen a partnership between the two groups. 



  The German stock-exchange operator said Tuesday that it would get a 1.5% stake in Payward as a result of the transaction in exchange for its investment, which is being made through an acquisition of existing shares from another investor. 



  Deutsche Boerse said the move highlights its commitment to its digital-asset strategy. The deal is expected to close in the second quarter, subject to regulatory approvals and other customary conditions, it added. 



  The investment builds on the existing relationship between Deutsche Boerse and Kraken, which teamed up in December last year, that aimed to bridge traditional financial markets and the digital asset economy. 



  Traditional exchange operators have sought to increase their exposure to digital assets. Kraken, meanwhile, last month became the first crypto firm to gain access to the Federal Reserve's core payment systems, in a move that allowed it to become more integrated into the mainstream financial system. 





  Write to Adria Calatayud at adria.calatayud@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 14, 2026 07:26 ET (11:26 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Fund-manager pessimism builds but not at 'close-your-eyes-and-buy' levels, says Bank of America
data.description:
By Jules Rimmer

Most pessimistic reading for almost a year reveals scope for recent trend reversal

Bank of America's monthly fund manager survey found increasing pessimism.

Bank of America's fund management survey, published Tuesday, is the most bearish for almost a year - but perhaps not pessimistic enough to signal an obvious rebound.

April's fund manager survey, authored as usual by chief investment strategist Michael Hartnett and his team of Anya Shelekhin, Myung-Jee Jong and Jessica Guo, hasn't generated the "close-your-eyes-and-buy" signal that other bearish surveys have in the past, partly because there hasn't been a complete capitulation in sentiment, they say.

For instance, Hartnett notes that 7 in 10 respondents do not forecast a recession, and there hasn't been the dash for cash one might have expected given the events and the uncertainty triggered by the closure of Hormuz. In fact, cash levels remained unaltered from March at 4.3%, still some way off the panicky highs of 4.8% in April 2025 and 6.3% in October 2022.

Hartnett's instinct is that even though risk sentiment has deteriorated markedly, investors, attuned to recoveries from geopolitically-induced sell-offs, have been reluctant to dump shares and are still long global stocks.

The key indicator of the survey is the reading of investor sentiment, a proprietary measure predicated on cash levels, equity allocation and growth expectations. The turndown in this signal is pronounced, but not as extreme as February 2022, April 2025 or 2008.

BofA Global FMS investor sentiment drops to lowest since June 2025.

The experience of very sharp rebounds from all those previous crises is clearly a guide as to why macro expectations and U.S. equity SPX performance have diverged so notably in the last six weeks or so. Even despite the worsening of growth forecasts, however, it's very apparent that a hard landing is still not the base case for most fund managers. Less than 10% forecast a hard landing for the economy this year and more than half of the 170 who replied still see a soft landing as the most likely scenario.

Global growth expectations drop to lowest since Aug 2025. The chart shows the net percentage expecting a stronger economy vs. the year-over-year change in the S&P 500.

One of the obvious losers from recent events is European stocks XX:SXXP. A net 21% of fund managers were overweight a month ago, but that cohort has now dropped to just 4%, as the region is viewed as the most vulnerable to higher energy prices, given its heavy import dependency.

Other notable findings from the poll were that the mean year-end oil price forecast is $84 per barrel, that oil and semiconductor stocks SOX are the most crowded trades right now and that expectations of a Democratic sweep in the midterm elections are mounting with more than a third of those polled making that call.

-Jules Rimmer

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  04-14-26 0727ET


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Markets Headlines at 7 AM ET: Stocks, Bonds Gain on U.S.-Iran Peace Talks Optimism | Global ...
data.description:
Stocks, Bonds Gain on U.S.-Iran Peace Talks Optimism

Global stocks rose and oil pulled back below $100 on optimism that peace talks to end the Middle East conflict would resume.

----

Global Oil Demand Heads for Contraction As Iran War Chokes Flows, IEA Says

The agency's forecast assumes oil and gas deliveries from the Middle East to international markets resume by midyear, though not to preconflict levels.

----

China's Export Momentum Slows Amid Iran War

The Middle East war is weighing on global demand and threatening a growth driver for Beijing.

----

$133 vs. $99. What Is the Real Price for a Barrel of Oil?

Prices for physical oil are becoming detached from what is implied in the financial markets.

----

Trump's Blockade on Iran in Strait Begins

The U.S. imposed a blockade on the Strait of Hormuz on Monday after peace talks with Iran collapsed over the weekend, risking another shock to a global economy battered by weeks of war.

----

BP Sees Exceptional Oil-Trading Result as Middle East War Fuels Volatility

The company expects upstream production to be broadly flat and that prices would result in a working-capital build and an increase in net debt.

----

The Bond Market Is 'Moving On' From the Iran War

One indicator shows markets are "moving on" from the war, says Barry Knapp, managing partner of Ironsides Macroeconomics.

----

Flimsier Cabinets and Fewer Windows: Home Builders Are Skimping on the Basics

Builders are pinching pennies to maintain profits in a stagnant housing market, choosing lower-end fixtures and synthetic over natural materials.

----

U.S. Oil Blockade Is Set to Boost American Exports-and Prices at the Pump

U.S. crude exports are poised to hit a record this month as more than 70 supertankers approach the Gulf Coast to load up on oil.

----

Canada's Carney Secures Majority Mandate

Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney cemented his hold on power after electoral victories Monday night and defections to his Liberal Party caucus over the past six months.

----

Singapore Central Bank Tightens Policy as It Braces for Slower Growth, Higher Prices

Singapore's central bank tightened monetary policy settings for the first time in over three years as it braces for the economic fallout from the war in the Middle East. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 14, 2026 07:15 ET (11:15 GMT)

data.description:
Oil Eases on U.S.-Iran Dialogue Hopes Despite Hormuz Blockade

Oil prices fell back below $100 a barrel on signs that the U.S. and Iran see scope to continue negotiations, but the U.S. blockade of the Strait of Hormuz was keeping crude prices elevated.

----

$133 vs. $99. What Is the Real Price for a Barrel of Oil?

Prices for physical oil are becoming detached from what is implied in the financial markets.

----

Global Oil Demand Heads for Contraction As Iran War Chokes Flows, IEA Says

The agency's forecast assumes oil and gas deliveries from the Middle East to international markets resume by midyear, though not to preconflict levels.

----

BP Sees Exceptional Oil-Trading Result as Middle East War Fuels Volatility

The company expects upstream production to be broadly flat and that prices would result in a working-capital build and an increase in net debt.

----

U.S. Oil Blockade Is Set to Boost American Exports-and Prices at the Pump

U.S. crude exports are poised to hit a record this month as more than 70 supertankers approach the Gulf Coast to load up on oil.

----

Blockade Plunges Oil Market Into Fresh Round of Turmoil

The loss of Iranian crude exports is likely to intensify a worldwide scramble for crude.

----

OPEC Crude Output Drops as Iran War Chokes Exports

OPEC's crude oil production plunged in March as the near-closure of the Strait of Hormuz forced key members to curb output and divert exports to alternative routes.

----

Bloom Energy Stock Jumps on Expanded Fuel Cell Deal for Oracle

Bloom's fuel cells will support Oracle projects in the U.S. amid demand for its AI and cloud-computing infrastructure.

----

GFL Environmental Expands Into Energy Sector With $4.6 Billion Secure Waste Acquisition

The deal pushes one of North America's largest waste-disposal companies into Canada's oilpatch.

----

Energy prices have probably peaked, according to Morgan Stanley's top strategist. Here's what that means for stocks.

Investors should be keeping a close eye on the difference between Brent and U.S. crude, which is sending a message that market worries over the crisis in Iran have peaked, says Morgan Stanley.

----

Baker Hughes to Sell Waygate Unit to Sweden's Hexagon For Around $1.45 Billion

Based in Germany, Waygate provides testing technologies to evaluate the structural integrity of materials across various industries.

----

The 'Real' Oil Price Is a Lot Higher Than It Looks

Spot crude prices measured in the North Sea are trading above $135 per barrel.

----

Oil just logged its worst week in 6 years as U.S.-Iran weekend talks raise a bigger question

Oil prices finished lower on Friday, with U.S. and global benchmark crude contracts posting their worst weekly percentage losses in nearly six years, as talks loomed between the U.S. and Iran.

data.title: Globalstar Shares Rise in Premarket on Amazon Buyout Report
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese 




  Globalstar shares surged in premarket trading Tuesday after a Bloomberg report said that Amazon.com is closing in on a takeover of the satellite operator, a move that would expand the tech giant's ambitions in space-based communications. 



  Shares traded 14% higher ahead of the morning bell at $82.80. 



  Amazon is in advanced talks to acquire Globalstar, according to the Bloomberg report, citing unidentified people familiar with the matter. 



  Neither Globalstar nor Amazon were immediately available for comment. 



   While the two companies haven't yet reached a final agreement, the unidentified people said that a deal could be announced as early as Tuesday. 



  Globalstar's stock has nearly tripled over the past 52 weeks, bringing its market cap to about $9.37 billion. 



  Apple owns a 20% stake in Globalstar, but Amazon wants Globalstar's satellite network and spectrum to accelerate its Project Kuiper satellite rollout and compete with SpaceX's Starlink. 





  Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 14, 2026 07:03 ET (11:03 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: J&J raises guidance for 2026 on strong start to the year
data.description:
By Jaimy Lee

The healthcare giant saw sales of medications, which include Darzalex and Tremfya, increase 11% in the first three months of 2026

Johnson & Johnson said its pharmaceutical sales rose 11.2% in the first quarter of 2026. (Photo by Jeff Schear/Getty Images for Johnson & Johnson)

Johnson & Johnson's immunology bet is paying off as the drugmaker gears up to market an innovative new psoriasis pill and had stronger-than-expected performance of another drug.

J&J spent the past few years slimming down its business to focus on six categories, one of which is immunology drugs. It's also planning to spin off its medical-devices unit after the success of divesting Kenvue, its consumer-health division, which is being acquired by Kimberly-Clark (KMB).

On Tuesday, the healthcare giant reported a 10% increase in sales in the first quarter, a period that's usually slow for drugmakers due to resetting deductibles for U.S. patients, among other factors. J&J also said it increased guidance for the year, expecting sales of $100.8 billion (compared with a previous estimate of $100.5 billion) and adjusted earnings per share of $11.55 (compared with $11.03).

The stock (JNJ) was flat in premarket trade.

The company said total sales rose 11.2% to $24.1 billion in the first quarter of 2026, up from $21.9 billion in the same quarter last year and beating the FactSet estimate of $23.6 billion. The bulk of that revenue came from its pharmaceuticals business, which generated $15.4 billion in sales for the quarter, compared with $13.9 billion a year ago. The average analyst estimate compiled by FactSet was $15.2 billion.

Key to J&J's future is Tremfya, an IL-23 inhibitor used to treat several autoimmune conditions, such as plaque psoriasis. Last September, the Food and Drug Administration approved a subcutaneous form of the drug for people with ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease, making it the first drug of its kind that can be administered at home for these conditions.

That gives it an advantage over AbbVie's (ABBV) Skyrizi, AbbVie's top-selling drug and one that requires patients to go to a clinic for an infusion when they start treatment.

Tremfya had $1.6 billion in sales in the first quarter, up from $956 million a year ago. The FactSet consensus was $1.4 billion.

J&J also received FDA approval last month for a new IL-23 inhibitor pill, Icotyde, for plaque psoriasis. That drug was developed out of a licensing deal it made with Protagonist Therapeutics (PTGX) in 2017. Icotyde could have peak sales of about $10 billion, according to Truist Securities analyst Srikripa Devarakonda. The company did not disclose any sales figures for Icotyde.

"This is going to be a transformational change for the treatment of these diseases," J&J CEO Joaquin Duato told investors in January.

Darzalex, which is J&J's multiple myeloma drug and top-selling product, brought in about $4.0 billion in revenue, up from $3.2 billion in the same three months last year. The FactSet estimate was $3.9 billion. The company wants its cancer drugs, led by Darzalex, to bring in $50 billion in sales by 2030.

J&J's medical devices unit had $8.6 billion in sales, matching the FactSet estimate of $8.6 billion.

Johnson & Johnson reported net income of $5.2 billion, which came in lower than the FactSet estimate of $6.5 billion. It also reported lower-than-expected net profit in the fourth quarter.

Adjusted earnings per share were $2.70. The FactSet estimate was for EPS of $2.68.

J&J's stock has gained 15% so far this year, while the S&P 500 SPX is up 1%.

-Jaimy Lee

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  04-14-26 0703ET


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Fink hails one of BlackRock's 'strongest starts to a year in our history'
data.description:
By Jamie Chisholm

The world's biggest asset manager beat earnings forecasts after a surge into its exchange-traded funds

BlackRock CEO Larry Fink speaks during a panel at the BlackRock Infrastructure Summit on March 11, 2026, in Washington, D.C.

BlackRock's shares rose early Tuesday after record inflows into its exchange-traded funds helped the world's biggest asset manager beat Wall Street's earnings expectations.

BlackRock Chairman and CEO Larry Fink said the company had delivered "one of the strongest starts to a year in our history" after it saw $130 billion of quarterly total net inflows, led by a record first quarter for iShares ETFs, along with active and private markets net inflows.

Demand for the company's investment products has remained strong as stock markets are showing great resilience despite the oil-price shock and war with Iran, with the S&P 500 SPX sitting only about 1% below record highs.

"We're engaged with clients across every channel, geography, and asset class," said Fink in a statement. "Our results and growing pipeline of business show that when clients are making big decisions about their portfolios, they are choosing BlackRock."

Revenue in the first quarter rose 27% from the same period a year ago to $6.7 billion. Adjusted earnings per share were $12.53 in the first quarter, up 11% from a year earlier, and higher than analysts' forecast of $10.84, according to FactSet.

BlackRock's assets under management were $13.9 trillion at the end of the first quarter, close to the record of a fraction over $14 trillion registered at the end of 2025.

BlackRock said it would undertake $450 million in share repurchases in the current quarter and deliver a 10% increase in quarterly cash dividend to $5.73 per share.

Investors welcomed the earnings report, with the stock up nearly 3% in premarket trading. Before the results, the stock was up 4.4% for the year to date, outperforming the S&P 500's gain of 0.6%.

The majority of analysts covering BlackRock have a buy rating on the stock, with a price target of $1,225, according to FactSet, which implies 16% upside from current levels.

-Jamie Chisholm

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  04-14-26 0655ET


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Johnson & Johnson Extends Royal Dividend Status With 3.1% Boost
data.description:
By Colin Kellaher 




  Johnson & Johnson's board has raised the quarterly dividend by 3.1%, to $1.34 from $1.30, making it 64 straight years that the healthcare giant has boosted its payout. 



  The new dividend, equal to $5.36 a year, represents an annual yield of about 2.25% based on Monday's closing price of $237.96, up from 2.19%. 



  With the increase, J&J maintains its place in the S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats Index, whose 69 members have paid a higher dividend for at least 25 straight years. 



  The increase also keeps J&J's spot on the smaller list of dividend kings--those who have raised their dividends annually for at least a half century. That group currently counts 57 members, according to website Sure Dividend. 



  The New Brunswick, N.J., company on Tuesday said the increased dividend is payable June 9 to shareholders of record May 26. 





  Write to Colin Kellaher at colin.kellaher@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 14, 2026 06:42 ET (10:42 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Why two Wall Street titans have turned bullish on U.S. stocks
data.description:
By Barbara Kollmeyer

Citigroup and BlackRock shift back to overweight on U.S. equities

U.S. stocks are ready to win again say Citigroup and BlackRock strategists.

While human and economic costs continue to mount from now 45-day Iran war, the U.S. stock market has moved on.

Rising in eight of the last nine trading days, the S&P 500 SPX is sitting just 1.32% away from its Jan. 27 record close, hovering at levels seen before the conflict began. While retail and institutional investors alike have stepped back from U.S. stocks as the conflict simmered, our call of the day flags big Wall Street U-turns from Citigroup and BlackRock.

At Citigroup, strategists are now recommending overweighting U.S. stocks from a prior neutral stance. They describe the shift as a tactical move - shelter in the highest quality and most defensive pockets of stocks, found in the U.S. That's as they see current Middle East tensions getting worse before they improve.

A team of strategists led by Beata Manthey said total damage from that conflict is "likely contained" from here. Citi argues that U.S. stocks can weather the storm. "At this point, we assume a headwind to consumption, inflation and Fed rate considerations, probably manageable so long as the current cease-fire trends to a wind down of hostilities over the next several weeks."

A second reason surrounds the cracks Citi now sees in what was a hopeful narrative of cyclicals and smaller companies starting to close the earnings gap with mega and large-cap tech leaders.

"Instead, the EPS contribution picture is narrowing again, with most of the incremental global earnings growth now expected to come from AI- and tech-linked segments," said Citi. That's as continued outperformance of rest-of-the-world stocks versus the U.S. are also hitting speed bumps, due to spiking commodity prices and global growth fallout.

"With cyclicals bearing the brunt of margin pressure and earnings uncertainty, the relative story shifts back toward the U.S., where earnings growth is increasingly dominated by resilient, high-margin, AI- and tech-enabled sectors," they said.

Until macro conditions stabilize and cyclical visibility improves, the rest of the world will struggle to catch up, reinforcing what U.S. stocks have to offer.

A final reason circles back to tech. While Mag 7 stocks have been a "sizable drag" on U.S. equity performance this year, they argue fundamentals remain strong. "The megacap tech cohort has seen consistent upward revisions to EPS and now trades at a meaningfully cheaper multiple vs. its history than the rest the U.S. index."

Citi is targeting an S&P 500 finish of 7,700 at year's end. Materials, healthcare and information technology are their sector overweights.

Elsewhere, strategists at the BlackRock Investment Institute, are also now recommending an overweight on U.S. stocks, after turning neutral a few weeks ago due to the Iran conflict. A team led by Jean Boivin offered two reasons for that, and note it's less of a shelter argument.

"First: evidence of actions that could reopen shipping traffic in the Strait of Hormuz. Second: signs that any lingering macro impact is contained. We have seen developments on both fronts," said Boivin, who is also recommending an overweight on emerging markets.

Expectations for corporate earnings in the U.S. and emerging markets are also rising for 2026, driven by the AI theme that also helps Taiwan and South Korea, says BlackRock. In the U.S., a forecast 80% boost for chip stock earnings this year is helping drive tech and overall earnings upgrades, they say.

"Tech's valuation premium has been eroded, with the 12-month forward valuation of the U.S. IT over other sectors at its lowest level since mid-2020. At the same time, the tech sector is now seen posting earnings growth of 43% in 2026, up from 26% last year," said Boivin and his team. They also like thematic opportunities such as defense.

BlackRock also addressed the elephant in the room - weren't they too cautious with their neutral stance? Their reply: the move "cost a few percentage points at the U.S. index level - a fair price to avoid a far costlier downside scenario materializing from worsening supply disruptions."

Read: Stock traders' 'WTF indicator' is going off. Why that is a bullish sign - at least for now.

The markets

U.S. stock futures (ES00) (YM00) (NQ00) are modestly up, as oil prices (CL.1) (BRN00) slip. 

   Key asset performance                                                Last       5d       1m      YTD     1y 
   S&P 500                                                              6886.24    4.15%    2.79%   0.60%   27.38% 
   Nasdaq Composite                                                     23,183.74  5.40%    3.62%   -0.25%  37.74% 
   10-year Treasury                                                     4.286      -1.40    8.30    11.40   -5.30 
   Gold                                                                 4787.6     2.38%    -4.46%  10.51%  48.37% 
   Oil                                                                  97.29      -13.60%  3.26%   69.47%  58.04% 
   Data: MarketWatch. Treasury yields change expressed in basis points

The buzz

The U.S. and Iran are reportedly considering a reboot of failed talks. Israel and Lebanon talks will begin in Washington on Tuesday.

Banking giants JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and Wells Fargo (WFC) each topped earnings expectations, as did Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), the healthcare giant.

United Airlines (UAL) reportedly pitched the government officials on a possible merger with American Airlines (AAL), whose shares are climbing.

The IEA's monthly oil report showed demand dropping by 80,000 barrels per day.

Producer prices are due at 8:30 a.m. The IMF's global economic outlook is due for release at 9 a.m.

Best of the web

Saudi Arabia is pressing U.S. to drop its Hormuz blockade.

He warned about the dangers of A.I. If only his father had listened.

As the Iran conflict squeezes global energy supplies, U.S. oil is gaining an unusual edge.

The chart

Energy prices shot up across the globe in March.

The chart from UBS shows how the Iran war has caused global energy inflation to spike by the most in 25 years. The bank tracked that metric for roughly 45 developed and emerging economies, and found 27 saw a median March jump of 5.5%, rivaling what was seen after Russia invaded Ukraine in March 2022.

Top tickers

These were the top-searched tickers on MarketWatch as of 6 a.m.:

   NVDA    Nvidia 
   TSLA    Tesla 
   INFY    Infosys 
   AMZN    Amazon 
   MSFT    Microsoft

Massive haul of Roman Empire crockery and swords hidden in Swiss lake.

-Barbara Kollmeyer
